EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that originated in the Gulf of Alaska rotated two fronts through northern California, eventually bringing snow levels to the 1500 foot level with large accumulations in the Sierra Nevada.  The second front was much stronger with 3-foot accumulations in the mountains and winds strong enough to cause white-out conditions.  Interstate 80 and other major roads where closed by transportation officials, and the US Forest Service issued avalanche warnings due the heavy, fast snowfall.

Heavy rains from the 18th to the 21st, including over 3-inch storm totals in the Sacramento area, caused small stream and urban flooding throughout the Sacramento Valley from Redding to Elk Grove.  5 inches of rain in the city of Oroville flooded 30 homes.

A strong surface high pressure area building into Nevada, and strong winds aloft, combined to produce powerful Santa Ana winds.Gusts of 100 mph were measured at La Canada with gusts to 75 mph over the Cajon Pass and Fremont Canyon.  Trees and power lines were damaged or blown down in many areas, knocking out power to some 200,000 residences, and igniting numerous small fires.  In Orange, fire spread from a flaming palm tree to a nearby two story duplex while at the Mall of Orange, twelve power poles snapped off.  In Montrose, a storage barn lost its roof and some businesses in the area had windows blown out.

A strong Pacific storm system brought rain and strong winds over a wide area of Southern California.  Highest winds occured in the northern deserts and in the mountains.  Burns Canyon in the East San Bernardino mountains logged a gust to 82 mph while Ranchita, in the San Diego County mountains had a gust to 61 mph.  In Shelter Valley, 45 miles northeast of San Diego, winds blew the roofs off many trailers.
A thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 70 mph at the Joshua Tree National Park visitor center.

A Pacific storm with abundant subtropical moisture brought more than 2 inches of water to the Sacramento area, causing widespread urban and small stream flooding. Winds to 35 mph downed trees, damaged a few homes and caused a number of power outages. Heaviest hit counties were Solano, Yolo, and Sacramento.

Strong winds associated with a Pacific cold front downed numerous trees and caused many power outages.  Peak wind speeds were 70 mph at Maple Creek around 7 am and 63 mph at Cooskie Mountain around 10 am.

After two days of periods of heavy rain, the Napa River began to rise quickly cresting above flood stage near the town of St. Helena at 1200 PST.  A 42 year old man drwoned when he slipped into the river in the city of Napa.
A heavy storm cell moved through the city of Modesto, causing some minor urban and small stream flooding on Dry Creek, a tributary of the Tuolumne River.

A cold, slow moving storm system produced a variety of weatheracross Southern California. Snow levels dropped to 1500 to 2000 feet. Mountain areas received 1 to 2 feet of snow while the Antelope Valley received up to 6 inches of snow. On the 25th, snow forced the closure of the Grapevine on Interstate 5for several hours. Thunderstorms developed in the cold, unstable air producing one-quarter to one-half inch hail. Coastal areasreceived betweeen .50 and 1.50 inches of rain.

Thunderstorms developed in the cold, unstable air producing one-quarter to one-half inch hail. Coastal areasreceived betweeen .50 and 1.50 inches of rain.

A waterspout came ashore near the mouth of the Smith River.  About two dozen trees were snapped off at the 20 foot level.  The largest trees were up to two feet in diameter.  A portion of one tree fell on the porch of a residence, demolishing the porch.
A second waterspout came ashore, passed between several homes, across a vacant lot and up the middle of a street before dissipating.  A wood fence in the vacant lot was damged.
Strong winds from a Pacific storm system spread across much of the North Coast.  Peak winds included:  51 mph at Shelter Cove, 57 mph at Maple Creek and 48 mph at the Eureka NWS office.

A storm that originated in the Gulf of Alaska rotated two fronts through northern California, eventually bringing snow levels to the 1500 foot level with large accumulations in the Sierra Nevada.  The second front was much stronger with 3-foot accumulations in the mountains and winds strong enough to cause white-out conditions.  Interstate 80 and other major roads where closed by transportation officials, and the US Forest Service issued avalanche warnings due the heavy, fast snowfall.

A very powerful Gulf of Alaska storm brought very cold air to the Sacramento valley, with snow reported as low as 500 feet.  WSO Redding had 5.4 inches accumulate.  Strong winds also accompanied the storm with gusts to 46kt as far south as Yuba County Airport with even higher gusts north of the airport.  In the Sierra Nevada, winds gusted to 60kt along the ridges.

Several creeks around Humboldt Bay quickly rose over their banks during a heavy rain event.  Numerous roads were closed due to water with considerable damage to the road surface.  The streams mainly affected were Jacoby, Freshwater and Salmon Creeks.
A cold front moving through San Bernadino County produced gusty winds which caused property damage in Barstow including downed power poles, tattered signs and broken windows.

A very powerful Gulf of Alaska storm brought very cold air to the Sacramento valley, with snow reported as low as 500 feet.  WSO Redding had 5.4 inches accumulate.  Strong winds also accompanied the storm with gusts to 46kt as far south as Yuba County Airport with even higher gusts north of the airport.  In the Sierra Nevada, winds gusted to 60kt along the ridges.

This Pacific storm carried abundant amounts of subtropical moisture and packed winds of 65kt in the Sierra Nevada.  Snowfall in the mountains measured as much as 3 feet.  Major highways across the Sierra Nevada were closed during the overnight hours.  Cold dry air trapped in the northern end of the Sacramento Valley, coupled with the warm moist air riding over the top of this cold air, caused a freak snowfall in the Redding area.  WSO Redding reported 6 inches of snow.  The surprise snowfall caused a number of vehicle accidents in the city of Redding and the surrounding area.

Strong Santa Ana winds caused widespread tree and crop damage and downed power lines.  Premature fruit was knocked off trees, causing many farmers to collect what they could from the ground. Downed power lines caused two minor fires which burned less than an acre.

Dense fog over the Tejon Summit in Kern County contributed to a 34 vehicle pileup on the southbound lanes of the Grapevine section of the I-5 despite the fact that traffic was being escorted by the California Highway Patrol.  Six people were sent to area hospitals with minor injuries.
Jacoby and Freshwater Creeks flooded southeast of Arcata.

A strong Pacific storm full of subtropical moisture left 12 to 18 inches of snow above the 7000 foot level in the Sierra Nevada.  Winds in the Sierra Nevada gusted to more than 50 kt.  At lower elevations, some minor flooding occurred in Solano County with valley locations reporting winds as strong as 30 kt.

Trees and power lines were damaged or blown down in many areas, knocking out power to some 200,000 residences, and igniting numerous small fires.  In Orange, fire spread from a flaming palm tree to a nearby two story duplex while at the Mall of Orange, twelve power poles snapped off.  In Montrose, a storage barn lost its roof and some businesses in the area had windows blown out.

Unstable conditions associated with a strong upper level low pressure trough set off showers, and gusty winds. There were reports of tree damage and a power outage.  Heavy snow fell above 4000 feet in the mountains with 6000 foot Laguna Peak reporting around one foot.

Mountain areas received 1 to 2 feet of snow while the Antelope Valley received up to 6 inches of snow. On the 25th, snow forced the closure of the Grapevine on Interstate 5for several hours. Thunderstorms developed in the cold, unstable air producing one-quarter to one-half inch hail. Coastal areasreceived betweeen .50 and 1.50 inches of rain.
Strong winds along the coastal strip blew throughout much of the day.  Trinidad Head peak wind gust was 49 mph.

A powerful Pacific storm produced strong winds in the mountains. Wind gusts from 50 to 70 mph were recorded in Big Bear, Green Valley, and Forest Falls. Numerous trees were downed and some structural damage was reported.

Mountain areas received 1 to 2 feet of snow while the Antelope Valley received up to 6 inches of snow. On the 25th, snow forced the closure of the Grapevine on Interstate 5for several hours. Thunderstorms developed in the cold, unstable air producing one-quarter to one-half inch hail. Coastal areasreceived betweeen .50 and 1.50 inches of rain.

A storm with origins in the Gulf of Alaska brought very cold air into Northern California, bringing snow to the 1000 foot level in the northern Sacramento Valley.  Paradise, a town in Butte County, had a 2-inch accumulation on the 27th.

A storm with origins in the Gulf of Alaska brought very cold air into Northern California, bringing snow to the 1000 foot level in the northern Sacramento Valley.  Paradise, a town in Butte County, had a 2-inch accumulation on the 27th.
At 8:45 am PDT, a Mission Beach lifegaurd reported a funnel cloud west of Point Loma.  The funnel cloud was moving north.
Rip currents and high surf kept lifegaurds busy during this warm ocean water event today.  19 city lifegaurds made 85 rescues, most of which were attributed to rip currents.

Lifegaurds were kept busy with rip currents and high surf this Sunday, even though the water temperature dropped to 59 degrees. 95 rescues were made with 42 San Diego city lifegaurds on duty.  For the most part, rip currents were to blame for the needed rescues.
5 to 8 inches of snow fell above the 1500 foot level.

Heavy rain caused many streams in Mendocino County to rise over their banks.  Highway 175 was closed in Hopland when Feliz Creek flooded.  Highway 128 was closed from Navarro to the coast by the Navarro River.

Two days of rainfall (Two consecutive daily accumulation records for the month of May in downtown Sacramento) brought river flows high enough to cause flooding, mainly in the Sierra Nevada foothills.  Near the town of Coloma on the south fork of the American River, 21 campers had to be rescued by boat when they were stranded by the rising water.  Elsewhere, sporadic surges of water closed schools and roads.

A strong Pacific storm full of subtropical moisture left 12 to 18 inches of snow above the 7000 foot level in the Sierra Nevada.  Winds in the Sierra Nevada gusted to more than 50 kt.  At lower elevations, some minor flooding occurred in Solano County with valley locations reporting winds as strong as 30 kt.

Passage of a severe thunderstorm with large hail over a wide area brought substantial damage to area agricultural in rural areas ESE of Fresno.  Damage was sustained by area peach, nectarine, almond orchards, and grapes.

With severe thunderstorms occurring in Central California during the early to mid afternoon hours, locally heavy rain brought deep ponding of water in several locations of north Fresno.  City intersections were flooded deep enough to strand cars.  One spotter report indicated 0.73" rain fell in a 25 minute period... another indicated 1.50 inches within 2 hours.

High wind from the SSW swept the western edge of the west Merced County town of Los Banos over a path length of 11/2 mile.  Very small structures, trees, and powerlines received damage.  Eyewitness accounts saw no funnel thus damage is attributed to downburst wind with associated thunderstorms rolling through the area in a "bow echo".  High wind lifted one 10'x10' small hay storage structure during heavy rain.  Another eyewitness watched powerlines being downed along with a small water utility structure.  Additional damage was caused to other small structure tin roofing.  This event could have been a very small F0 tornado but evidence appears to support thunderstorm wind.

CZ_NAME: SALINAS VALLEY/INTERIOR MONTEREY COUNTY/SAN BENITO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm rain on top of snow brought large amounts of water off the mountains in Yosemite National Park (YNP).  The resulting water surge put the Merced River above flood stage at the Sentinel Bridge in the Park.  Southern Sierra Nevada spotter 24-hour rainfall reports on the morning of Thursday, May 16, ranged from 2.0 - 2.5 inches.  Snow levels were above 10,000 feet in the Southern Sierra Nevada which added to the rainfall runoff with snow melt.  The Merced River crested at 12.0 feet which is 1.8 feet above the stage when minor flooding begins.  Damage estimates are in the $2-3M range.

A line of thunderstorms moving through Central California in the Merced, Madera, and Fresno County area brought damage to trees, powerlines, and small structures from downburst winds.  Power was lost to 7-9000 Fresno residents.  Damage estimates to power conveyance systems probably was in the neighborhood of $150K for the Merced, Madera, Fresno County area.

This tornado briefly touched down in an agricultural area near the city of Lodi. It snapped a utility pole and brought down a 6-foot diameter oak tree, which crashed into the roof of a two story home.
This weak tornado briefly touched down in a residential section of downtown Lodi.  It damaged approximately 40 trees and did minor damage to residential roofs.

On the eve of Memorial Day weekend, a winter type storm produced hail in the northern half of San Diego county.  The only place that reported large hail was the city of Fallbrook.  A spotter from the California Fire Department reported heavy rain and one inch diameter hail.  1.84 inches of precipitaion feel in Fallbrook.  Other areas in the San Diego reported hail but mostly in the one quater to one half size range.  Isolated flooding and some traffic accidents resulted.  About 20,000 electric customers lost power at different times on this Friday.  Also, snow flurries were reported in the mountians above 4000 feet.

A strong South Pacific storm, south of Tahiti, produced heavy surf along the beaches of Southern California. Average surf conditions of 7 to 10 feet with isolated sets to 12 feet were reported. Theheavy surf broke a 40 foot high splash guard at the Point Mugu Naval Base, causing minor flooding in several buildings. Also,lifeguards reported over 500 rescues at Southern California beaches.

A strong South Pacific storm, south of Tahiti, produced heavy surf along the beaches of Southern California. Average surf conditions of 7 to 10 feet with isolated sets to 12 feet were reported. Theheavy surf broke a 40 foot high splash guard at the Point Mugu Naval Base, causing minor flooding in several buildings. Also,lifeguards reported over 500 rescues at Southern California beaches.
Strong pressure gradients across the West produced gusty winds over Northwest California.  Peak gusts at 1 pm PST were 41 mph at Trinidad Head and 39 mph at Cooskie Mountain.

A late spring cold front produced gust winds across Northwest California.  The peak wind reported was 58 mph at the 2500 foot level of Bunker Hill at 10 am PST.  Other reported winds were 42 mph at Trinidad Head at 730 am PST and 43 mph at the NWS office near downtown Eureka at 10 am PST.

Areas of dense coastal fog shrouded much of the Northwest California coast and river valleys.  Visibilities were reduced to near zero in Eureka and around Humboldt Bay and were down to 1/8 mile in Hydesville.

The staff of Orestimba High School in the northwest portion of the town witnessed the small tornado approaching from the south-southeast.  It ripped the roof off a small shed and destroyed an athletic field sign.
Large ocean swells generated by a South Pacific storm slammed into southwest facing coastlines.  Numerous boats in Point Arena harbor were damaged or loosened from their moorings.

A line of severe thunderstorms, associated with a bow echo indicated on the Sacramento WSR-88D at the time, produce straight-line damage in the San Joaquin River delta. Heaviest damage occurred to a boat marina (the source of the estimated value of damage).  A local park also sustained damage in the form of 30 uprooted oak trees of more than 2 feet in diameter. On nearby Interstate 5, two tractor trailers were blown over on the highway, which runs north-south, by the strong westerly winds.

A spring storm brought high winds to both the Sacramento Valley and northern Sierra Nevada mountain range.  In the city of Folsom, CA, a 100-foot oak tree crashed into an apartment building, damaging 6 units.  In Tuolumne County, 40000 customers lost electricity.  Numerous motor vehicle accidents occurred in the mountains, including a tractor-trailer collision that released hazardous materials and temporarily closed Interstate 80.

A spring storm brought high winds to both the Sacramento Valley and northern Sierra Nevada mountain range.  In the city of Folsom, CA, a 100-foot oak tree crashed into an apartment building, damaging 6 units.  In Tuolumne County, 40000 customers lost electricity.  Numerous motor vehicle accidents occurred in the mountains, including a tractor-trailer collision that released hazardous materials and temporarily closed Interstate 80.

A strong South Pacific storm, south of Tahiti, produced heavy surf along the beaches of Southern California. Average surf conditions of 7 to 10 feet with isolated sets to 12 feet were reported. Theheavy surf broke a 40 foot high splash guard at the Point Mugu Naval Base, causing minor flooding in several buildings. Also,lifeguards reported over 500 rescues at Southern California beaches.

A small F0 tornado occurred with the passage of a severe thunderstorm through Central Fresno County.  Little damage was done by the tornado although considerable crop damage resulted from hail and heavy rain with the thunderstorm.
Large hail occurred with the passage of a severe thunderstorm that also generated a small F0 tornado.  Damage was sustained by agricultural area grapes, almonds, and plums.
Severe thunderstorms with accompanying heavy rain and hail damaged sensitive stone fruit crops in Central Fresno County.

Thunderstorm high wind overturned a tractor-trailer as it was east-bound on State Highway 152 8 miles east of Los Banos.  Numerous reports of high wind occurred in the vicinity around the time period of this event.
The Elkhorn Country Club experienced numerous snapped trees as this tornado developed from a bow echo that was evident on the Sacramento WSR-88D.
Spotter report of 3/4 inch hail in rural Madera County. Path length and width unknown.  Crop damage estimated.

A small, F0 tornado caused damage 4 miles SSE of Tulare in rural Tulare County.  One eyewitness observed an approaching small, rope funnel prior to the occurrence of damage to dairy sunshades, house roof, house windows, and block wall.
A weak tornado touched down on the Swenson Park golf course, uprooting a number of large trees.

Hot and dry conditions, produced by offshore winds, continued to fuel a fire in the Los Padres National Forest. The fire, which ignited in late April, destroyed one mobile home and scorched numerous citrus and avocado orchards. In addition, several firefighters were injured.

On Mother's Day, hot Santa Ana winds helped fuel a brush fire near the University of California at Riverside. The fire burned over 300 acres, including property owned by Kappa Alpha fraternity. Three cars and two mobile homes were destroyed.

In the wake of a cold front, strong winds developed below passes and canyons of Santa Barbara county. Sustained northerly winds 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 55 mph were reported. Numerous branches were knocked down into power lines, leaving over 6000 residents of Santa Barbara without electricity for several hours.

In western Merced County high wind damaged trees and structures beginning 31/2 miles south of Gustine and ended with powerline and structure damage within the town itself.  Only tree, very small structures, and some roof damage was apparent from 31/2 miles south of town to 2 miles south of town.  About 2 miles south of Gustine and within a 1/2 mile path, a large, well constructed, 60'x60' wood barn, an historical slate rock building, trees, and 40'x100', wood barn were flattened.  Eyewitness accounts attribute the damage to high wind only.

The California Highway Patrol received a report that a motorist from a cellular phone that a tornado occurred southeast of the rural town of El Nido in Merced County.  Pacific Gas and Electric did have to repair a pole in that vicinity.  Residents reported that high wind and rain immediately preceded a period of calm and no rain.  Whistling, humming, and then subsequent damage occurred to roofs, powerlines, and trees while residents hid in their houses.

High wind from the southwest flattened an older barn 3 miles SW of Los Banos in west Merced County per eyewitness reports.  A California Division of Forestry and Fire Protection anemometer measured wind in the hour prior to 1500 PST at a location 10 miles west of Los Banos at 52 knots.  This report would match with the line of thunderstorms developing along the San Joaquin Valley west side at that time.
Out of an estimated 295,000 visitors to the San Diego County beaches, 366 rescues were made.  Many were blamed on strong rip currents.  One 10-year-old San Diego boy drowned at Mission Beach.
Strong thunderstorm winds at Twentynine Palms Airport broke a glider loose from its mooring.  The runaway sailplane damaged a hanger, another glider and destroyed itself in the process.
Nearby thunderstorms produced a gust to 58 mph at Barstow/Daggett Airport.  No damage was reported.
A three hour rain event, sustained by redeveloping thunderstorms over the New York Mountains, resulted in a three to four foot deep river that washed out a secondary road near Barnwell.

A stationary thunderstorm over the Trinity Alps produced heavy rain at the headwaters of Canyon Creek and the North Fork of the Trinity River.  A Trinity County Sheriff's Department sergeant reported a six foot wall of water in Canyon Creek near Junction City.  US Forest Service rangers reported water levels 10 feet above base flow at the head of Canyon Creek.  Hikers in the Trinity Alps Wilderness Area reported 1/2 inch hail covering the ground to a depth of six inches.  Quick action by the Trinity County Sheriff's Department cleared all people from the path of the flood which resulted in no injuries or loss of life.
Lightning damaged a water fountain and underground power cables leading to it.

The heaviest snow in many years caused a number of problems in the Shasta-Cascades region and in the Sierra Nevada range.  8-foot snow accumulations at the higher elevations in a 36-hour timeframe came within half a foot of the record at Kirkwood Ski Resort (Amador/El Dorado counties).  A storm total of 8 feet fell in the Lake Tahoe area.  4-foot accumulations in 30 hours occurred at the 3000 to 4000-foot elevations.  Heavy snow fell as low as 1500 feet, and lighter accumulations fell as low as 500 feet MSL.  The heavy snow pulled down numerous trees, taking power lines with them.  About 120,000 people in the Sierra Nevada and its foothills lost power for several days, with some getting back power late Christmas Eve, and most regaining power by the day after Christmas.  The snow closed most roads and several major highways in the region, including state route 70, Interstate 80 (closed nearly two days), and US Route 50 (closed nearly 3 days).  The passenger train "California Zephyr" (Amtrak's Oakland to Chicago route) derailed in the Sierra Nevada near the town of Blue Canyon.  Falling trees brought down a water flume in Tuolumne county and stopped power to a water pump that serviced much of Amador county; so county residents also went without running water for several days.  Small avalanches at a ski resort injured two people. The storm also caused two fatalities.  One man died from hypothermia when he got lost cross-country skiing in the blizzard condtions.  A woman died near Lassen Lodge when her car plummeted off a 150-foot cliff from state highway 36.  These heavy snows would not melt until the end of the year, when they contributed to some of the worst flooding in state history.

A Pacific storm produced heavy rains and strong wind in the Sacramento and northern San Joaquin valleys. Snow accumulations in the mountains measured 10 inches at Lake Tahoe and 2.5 to 3 feet of snow at higher elevations. Minor flooding occurred in Solano county and in the city of Turlock (Stanislaus county), and the rain caused numerous motor vehicle accidents, but the chief effect was power outages--36,300 homes and businesses, mainly in the Sierra Nevada foothills. The figure also includes 12,000 homes and businesses in Stockton (San Joaquin county).  The KCPM television station lost a transimitter in Tehama county.  State Highway 88 through the Sierra Nevada mountains had to be closed due to the excessive snow.

Pacific storm with gust in the 60 to 70 mph range hit the entire Bay Area. The soil was quite saturated from earlier rains so many trees were blown across power lines leaving a total of 14,000 customers with power including the Berkley Police department .

San Francisco had 2.6" of rain that flooded Us 101 and the parking lots at Candlestick Park causing massive traffic jams of people trying to get to 49ers game. Redwood City had 2.5", Oakland 2.34", and San Rafeal 2.09". Metro Muni station on Van Ness was flooded. 394 traffic accidents were recorded from 6 AM to 4 PM 0n 3/17/96. power was cut to 43,600 people.
9.15" of rain fell in about 30 hours. Big Sur 8.05" fell in the sam period. Mudslides closed Hwy 1 in the south part of county.

Widespread flooding of streams and rivers.  Most rivers were still rising as the year ended.  Some of the streams and rivers that flooded were:  Janes Creek, Deans Creek, Klamath River at Turwar, Van Duzen River at Grizzly Creek, Eel River at Fernbridge and Eel River near Scotia.  Damage estimates will be included in January 1997 totals.
The Garcia River flooded Highway 1 near Point Arena, the Navarro River flooded 12 miles of Highway 128 near the junction with Highway 1 and 10 Mile River flooded homes 8 miles north of Fort Bragg.
The Truckee Ranger Station reported 14 inches of snow over a 24 hour period.

Lake Tahoe ski resorts reported heavy snow amounts over a 24 hour period.  Some significant reports were 24-36 inches at Mt. Rose, 20-32 inches at Kirkwood, and 17-23 inches at Heavenly.  Other ski resorts gave 12 hour amounts since the previous day such as 14 inches at Alpine Meadows and 10 inches at Northstar.
A weather spotter in Doyle measured a wind gust of 50 kts.

A strong Pacific storm produced heavy rainfall across Southern California. One to three inches of rain fell overthe Southland. Mudslides closed Interstate 5 near Sun Valley with over four feet of mud.

Strong Santa Ana winds pummelled Southern California. Sustained winds speed reached 45 to 55 MPH withgusts up to 95 MPH through passes and canyons. Numerous damage occurred, including many downed powerlines which left thousands without electricity. A tractor trailer driver was injured with the winds knocked his  truckoff of Highway 118 in the Santa Susana Pass. In the Lakeview Terrace area, One 82 year-old man was killedand his 28 year-old daughter-in-law injured when a pine tree branch fell on their minivan.
A line of severe thunderstorms affected the North Coast of Santa Barbara county. Winds, associated with the storms, were as high as 77 MPH.

76 mph wind at Maple Creek.

Strong winds developed again along the coast from Cape Mendocino north.  A gust blew a board onto piping at a power generating facility in Fairhaven.  The pipe ruptured spilling 800 gallons of sulphuric acid.  The same winds helped disperse the fumes.  40 mph winds were reported 9S Honeydew and 2.5NW Klamath.  Cooskie Mountain had 73 mph winds shortly after midnight.
Four inches of snow fell overnight in Trinity County 17E Bridgeville.
73 mph wind at Cooskie Mountain and 51 mph at Trinidad Head.

Numerous trees and power lines were downed in high winds.  Cooskie Mountain reached a peak gust 106 mph at 606 am on the 29th.  Some other speeds:  46 mph at Eureka NWS office, 65 mph 5E Eureka, 56 mph Orick, 45 mph 7S Honeydew.
Numerous trees and power lines downed in high winds.  Some peak gusts:  86 mph at Maple Creek, 58 mph at Cow Mtn Ridge and 59 mph at Crazy Peak.
A surfer was swept out by heavy surf and slmmed into a cliff at Moonstone Beach near Trinidad.
Peak wind at Anthony Peak 69 mph.

Gusty Santa Ana winds redeveloped across parts of Southern California resulting in spotty reports of downed trees and power poles.  In Rancho Cucamonga, winds toppled a 500,000 kilovolt electric tower, sparking a fire that burned 250 acres, forcing the evacuation of 80 homes.  Elsewhere across the four county area, downed trees and power poles resulted in minor damage and power outages to thousands of homes and businesses.

A Pacific storm left accumulations on average of six inches in the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada.  The accumulation and slippery conditions created enough accidents to temporarily close US Highway 50 through the mountain chain.

A major winter storm system produced significant rainfall and blizzard like conditions across Southern California.Rainfall totals up to two inches were measured along the coast while mountain areas below 6000 feet receivedup to 5 inches of rain. Mudslides closed Interstate 14 near Santa Clarita. At elevations above 6000 feet, strongwinds 40 to 50 MPH combined with snow to produce blizzard-like conditions. Snow accumulations up to six inches were reported at higher elevations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front sweeping through Southern California, was accompanied by strong, gusty winds and bursts of moderate to heavy precipitation.  As the front moved through  the San Gorgonio Pass, a short lived tornado spun up at Cabazon.  The twister was noticed by firemen near the corner of Dell and Lemon, and moved northeastward only about 700 feet before dissipating.  On it's short path, it lifted a 5 ton mobile home and deposited it 30 feet from it''s foundation with roof and contents removed.  Six other mobile homes suffered minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in the town of Mammoth Lakes reported 16 inches of snow in about a 12 hour period (8,000 foot elevation).  About 2 feet of snow had fallen in Mammoth Lakes since the storm began.  Snow was falling at the rate of 1-2 inches an hour.
The Mono County Sheriff's Office reported 8-10 inches in the town of Bridgeport, 18 inches at Devil's Gate, and 24 inches at Conway Summit.  All the snow had fallen in a 12-24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Santa Ana winds once again ravaged Southern California. Sustained northeast winds 40 to 50 MPH withgusts up to 70 MPH were reported. Thousands were left without power as the winds snapped power lines. Inaddition, blowing debris and downed trees caused widespread damage.

A major winter storm system produced significant rainfall and blizzard like conditions across Southern California.Rainfall totals up to two inches were measured along the coast while mountain areas below 6000 feet receivedup to 5 inches of rain. Mudslides closed Interstate 14 near Santa Clarita. At elevations above 6000 feet, strongwinds 40 to 50 MPH combined with snow to produce blizzard-like conditions. Snow accumulations up to six inches were reported at higher elevations.

Downburst winds damaged the roof of a house, heavily damaged a barn, and destroyed a mobile home in this small town in southern Sacramento county.  6000 homes and businesses lost power in the city of Elk Grove, where some light fixtures for an athletic stadium collapsed in the wind.

A cold front sweeping through the San Bernardino and Riverside Mountains generated strong winds and heavy, wet snow in elevations above 6000 feet.  Snow began falling during the early morning hours of the 22nd, and increased in intensity, along with the winds, until blizzard conditions were reported shortly after 7:00 AM. In the Big Bear Valley, 40 to 50 mph winds combined with heavy snow to snap trees and power lines, and cause severe visibility restrictions for several hours.  By the early afternoon, the storm had diminished, leaving up to one half foot of snow in some areas.

Strong winds developed over the high desert areas in the wake of a cold front, under increasing winds aloft.  Sustained winds of 35 mph, with gusts peaking around 70 mph were logged in the Lucerne Valley, locally restricting visibilities to less than 30 feet.  The winds destroyed several sheds and porches, and caused some roof damage.  Downed power poles severed electric service to many areas during the morning hours.

Gusty Santa Ana winds redeveloped across parts of Southern California resulting in spotty reports of downed trees and power poles.  In Rancho Cucamonga, winds toppled a 500,000 kilovolt electric tower, sparking a fire that burned 250 acres, forcing the evacuation of 80 homes.  Elsewhere across the four county area, downed trees and power poles resulted in minor damage and power outages to thousands of homes and businesses.
Peak wind at Kneeland 45 mph.

Heavy rains and snow pack melted by a subtropical airmass began on the 26th, creating flooding on the upper Sacramento River.  The flooding began on the 29th at the Tehama Bridge gage (Tehama county).  The flooding spread downstream to the Vina-Woodson bridge (Tehama county) very early on the 31st and to Hamilton City (along the Butte-Glenn county border) on the afternoon of the 31st.  The overflow inundated several mobile home parks along the river and closed local roads.  500 people were evacuated in the town of Tehama.  The flooding continued through the first week of the New Year.

Strong Santa Ana winds toppled trees and electric poles, smashed windows, blew out signs, and knocked out power to tens of thousands across scattered areas of Southern California.  In Orange County, gusts at Fremont Canyon were measured as high as 111 mph.  Two deaths occured in San Bernardino County, both in Fontana.  A 28 year old man died when a live powerline was blown on him and another 82 year old man died when a pine tree branch fell onto his van, breaking his neck.  Two minor injuries occurred in Huntington Central Park in Orange County, when a 60 foot tree blew down.  Also in San Diego County, one minor injury resulted when a tree crashed into a car traveling on Highway 78, just east of Ramona.  Just below Crestline, in the San Bernardino Mountains, a radio tower was blown down and the roof blown off the transmitter building.  Mira Loma was particularly hard hit with many downed trees and 50 power poles.  Two tractor-trailer rigs were flipped on the I-15 near Devore, closing this major north-south highway for 15 hours, while a pickup with camper was blown over on Highway 70 near Santa Isabel.  Several cars were smashed under falling trees, up to 9 feet in diameter, and downed power lines sparked fires that were fanned by the gusty winds.  In Rialto where winds were clocked at 92 mph, sparks from downed power lines started a blaze that gutted a tile production plant.  In San Diego County, the winds knocked  over one and one half million pounds of avocadoes from the trees, a large percentage of which could not be marketed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Santa Ana winds once again ravaged Southern California. Sustained northeast winds 40 to 50 MPH withgusts up to 70 MPH were reported. Thousands were left without power as the winds snapped power lines. Inaddition, blowing debris and downed trees caused widespread damage.

The tornado warning on this event was issued on the basis of radar signature only as land line reports of the tornado at the base were not received until later.  A meso-cyclone was depicted by the HNX WSR-88D on the 1429 PST volume scan.  This was one of two tornadoes from this same cell as determined by storm investigation.  This tornado was observed to be very slow moving with cell movement northwest to southeast and a path length of 1 mile or so over barren ground in the mid-field of Lemoore Naval Air Station.  The funnel with this tornado was observed to have completely dissipated by weather observers ... thus the breaking of the event into two separate tornado reports.

Flooding reported along Canyon Creek and the Trinity River near Junction City.  Flooded also near Carrville and Weaverville.  The driver of a car swept into Swift Creek at Trinity Center was washed away and drowned when he tried to exit the vehicle.

A Pacific storm produced heavy rains and strong wind in the Sacramento and northern San Joaquin valleys. Snow accumulations in the mountains measured 10 inches at Lake Tahoe and 2.5 to 3 feet of snow at higher elevations. Minor flooding occurred in Solano county and in the city of Turlock (Stanislaus county), and the rain caused numerous motor vehicle accidents, but the chief effect was power outages--36,300 homes and businesses, mainly in the Sierra Nevada foothills. The figure also includes 12,000 homes and businesses in Stockton (San Joaquin county).  The KCPM television station lost a transimitter in Tehama county.  State Highway 88 through the Sierra Nevada mountains had to be closed due to the excessive snow.

The Inyo County Sheriff reported two tractor-trailer trucks and a motor home blown over by very strong winds in the Owens Valley.  This forced a closure of state highway 395 near Olancha for a few hours.
Heavy rain and large amounts of small hail cause a roof support to give way, taking a portion of the roof.
Low visibilities were the major factor in the collision of two vehicles at an intersection in the county.

Strong Santa Ana winds pummelled Southern California. Sustained winds speed reached 45 to 55 MPH withgusts up to 95 MPH through passes and canyons. Numerous damage occurred, including many downed powerlines which left thousands without electricity. A Pacific storm left accumulations on average of six inches in the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada.  The accumulation and slippery conditions created enough accidents to temporarily close US Highway 50 through the mountain chain.

Damage was observed in South Lake Tahoe from strong gusty winds during the morning hours.  Trees fell on a couple of homes, causing considerable damage.  From surrounding data, winds were estimated in the 50-60 kt range.

This was the major event of the day in the CWFA.  Storm damage to the Lemoore Naval Air Station administrative section was substantial but no injuries occurred.  Again, the tornado warning was issued on the basis of radar interpretation as land line reports were received late into the office.  A meso-cyclone was depicted by the HNX WSR-88D for 4 volume scans from 1429 to 1447 PST.  Damage included roof removal of the base recyling center, wind damage to roofs on several administrative structures, powerlines and poles, and fixed structures (static display aircraft).

Associated with the tornadic supercell at Lemoore N.A.S. was hail damage to vehicles.  Side and front windows on vehicles were smashed by large hail stones during storm passage.  Post storm survey determined that hail stones reached up to 2.5 inches in diameter.

Path of this tornado was over barren ground with no damage reported.  It was observed to travel its 2 miles and marked by dust and vegetative debris.  A funnel cloud had been reported to have dissipated by FAA Tower personnel before the delayed report of this tornado was received.  Rotation not detected by the HNX WSR-88D as the radar beam at this location was approx. 7000 AGL.  Reflectivity maximum in the area was at 60 dBz.

Rains of 2 to 4.5 inches across El Dorado county during the early morning brought many small streams to above flood stage.  On Deer Creek near the city of Cameron Park, 10 homes were evacuated.  Further downstream, in eastern Sacramento county, the creek inundated the local area, damaging a restaurant in an historic building in the town of Sloughhouse.  The city of El Dorado's post office ended up with 4 feet of water inside the building from Slate Creek.

A powerful winter storm pummeled the Mono County area with heavy snow.  The most noteworthy snowfall accumulations were from early Saturday morning (21st) to late Saturday afternoon and also from late Saturday afternoon to Sunday afternoon.  At Mammoth Lakes, 20 inches of snow fell from early Saturday morning to late Saturday afternoon (12-18 hour period).  Also, Tom's Place (southeast of Mammoth Lakes) reported 12 inches of snow in the same time frame.  The Mammoth Lakes area received an additional 20-30 inches of snow from late Saturday afternoon to Sunday afternoon (22nd).  The storm total ending early Monday morning (23rd) was 53 inches at Mammoth Lakes with 72 inches at the Mammoth Ski Resort.

Winds to 75mph reported near los Gatos in Santa Clara County. winds gusting to 85 mph on Mt Diablo. in Alameda Co.

Seven inches of rain fell on Benlomand in the Santa Cruz mountains causing the San Lorenzo river to rise to near flood. many tree were toppled by the combination of soaking rain and winds. winds to 45 mph were reported.

3.23" of rain were recorded at Corrolitos is on the  Corrolitos Creek. The Corrolitos and Salsipuedes Creeks exceeded their banks and flooded many agricultural fields including Strawberries, Lettuce and orchards. It closed many roads in the area and contributed to a large rise in the Pajaro River near Watsonville. 10 roads in the area were closed and 300 people were evacuated along the Corrolitos Creek.

Pacific storm brought widespread street and highway closures due to high water. HWy US 101 was closed by water over the road about 10 streets were under water. Many of the lush agriculutral fields were under water, but, most were dormant for the winter.

A wildfire...sparked by arcing power lines...burned over 13,000acres of land between Calabasas and Malibu. Two seperate SantaAna wind events...with wind gusts up to 70 mph...helped spread theflames toward the ocean through the week. Six homes...four mobilehomes and three vehicles were destroyed. Sixteen emergencypersonnel were injured...including fourteen firefighters.

Strong Santa Ana winds...gusting up to 71 mph...knocked down apower transformer in the Lemon Heights area. As a result...a firebegan in the community. By the time the fire was extinguished...twenty-nine homes were destroyed.

This Pacific storm caused widespread damage through the entire cwa. Oakland had 2.02" of rain in the preceding 24 hours. This caused an earthen holding pond to rupture and the ensuing flash flood of mud, water, and debris swept across I580 a major commute route. it created a four foot deep lake a quarter mile long. Six cars were trapped in the muck but no injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter near Lake Shastina reported 68 mph gust

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, snow melt, and large reservoir releases in anticipation of more rain caused the Tuolumne River to rise about one foot above flood stage.

2.96" of rain in 11 hours at Monterey, Seaside had 2.5"and Marina had 2.12". Streets and intersections were flooded in Monterey, Del Rey Oaks,Pacific Grove, Carmel Highlands, and Carmel. HWY 101 n OF Saslinas was flooded.

Strong Santa Ana winds pummelled Southern California. Sustained winds speed reached 45 to 55 MPH withgusts up to 95 MPH through passes and canyons. Numerous damage occurred, including many downed powerlines which left thousands without electricity. A tractor trailer driver was injured with the winds knocked his  truckoff of Highway 118 in the Santa Susana Pass. In the Lakeview Terrace area, One 82 year-old man was killedand his 28 year-old daughter-in-law injured when a pine tree branch fell on their minivan.

3.01" of rain fell in the usually dry region of Orinda. More than 2" of rain blanketed the Bay area from Vallejo and Marin South to Redwood City. Causing widespread urban street and Hwy flooding. 13,000 customers were without power.

Pacific storm brought widespread high winds to the Bay Area. Rainfall was generally less than an inch but the soil was saturated so many trees were blown down. Power outages occurred at 87,500 customers. There was urban street and highway flooding. two major Cable TV providers were knocked off line by the storm.

Third system in a large Pacific storm system brought winds to 60 mph and hvy precip to nrn ptn of San Francisco Bay area. Many trees were blown down and into power lines. 21,000 people were without power.

This storm cut a 20 yard wide quartrer mile path of distruction through the north side of Napa. A jogger was slammed into a fence but reported only minor injuries. The storm was spotted by the Napa police.

Wide spread heavy precipatation from Pacific storm. SFO 1.4" santa Cruz 2.0", Boulder Creek 2.3" Kentfield 2.0". Large pine tree fell through home in Oakland due to the wet soil and wind in the storm. power was out to as many as 3500 people in the area. Traffic accidents were nearly double normal with 200 during the afternoon commute.

After nearly 3 inches of rain in the fairly arid hills of Alameda County a series of levees. Water and mud up to 6 ft deep rushed down the street and three adjjoining cul de sacs. Submergin cars and several residences leaving 6 inches to 2 feet of Muck.

2 males were drowned when a 48 foot Crab Fishing boat capsized about one mile off of Point Bonita in 15 to 20 ft waves and winds of 35 mph. 1 Fisher man swam to shore and was treated for hyperthermia.

A Pacific storm produced heavy rains and strong wind in the Sacramento and northern San Joaquin valleys. Snow accumulations in the mountains measured 10 inches at Lake Tahoe and 2.5 to 3 feet of snow at higher elevations. Minor flooding occurred in Solano county and in the city of Turlock (Stanislaus county), and the rain caused numerous motor vehicle accidents, but the chief effect was power outages--36,300 homes and businesses, mainly in the Sierra Nevada foothills. The figure also includes 12,000 homes and businesses in Stockton (San Joaquin county).  The KCPM television station lost a transimitter in Tehama county.  State Highway 88 through the Sierra Nevada mountains had to be closed due to the excessive snow.

This storm began over the ocean as a water spout that moved in land. It distroyed a large barn  and 1o large eucalytus trees, and 5 telephone poles. It also ripped the roof off another house. Afarmer was in the barn when it was blown over but he was not injured. At least two other waterspouts were reported as well as heavy rain and small hail in the tomales bay area.
Lightning started the Rogge wildfire in the Stanislaus National Forest along the Tuolumne River.  The wildfire took two weeks to get under control, burning 23,200 acres.

Lightning caused a wildfire known as the Ackerson Complex fire.  The fire burned 61,070 acres.  August was the worst month of the year for wildfire in California.  In 1996, 232,984 acres burned throughout the state, or 90 percent greater than the normal acreage of 122,362 acres.

Lightning started the "Keystone" fire that burned in the jurisdiction of the Tuolumne-Calaveras Ranger Unit of the California Department of Forestry. This wildfire destroyed 20 homes and damaged 7 others. It burned 7000 acres.

Police determined that low visibility in the rain was the main factor as a driver missed a stop sign as he approached a road "T" and drove the car into a concrete abutment.  The heavy rain also destroyed 60,000 books in the Shasta county library when the roof sprung a leak.
Highway 1 north of Point Arena was flooded by the Garcia River, Highway 175 flooded near Hopland and Highway 128 was flooded from the Highway 1 junction to milepost 12.5.
Very heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused Hatch Creek to overflow its banks.  A 70 year old woman drowned as she attempted to drive her car through a flooded section of roadway.

This month continued to show much above normal rainfall reflected by the monthly rain total of 4.26 inches as measured at Fresno ... 3.68 inches had occurred by the 29th. This makes December 96 the fourth wettest on record.  Adjacent mountain area precipitation and snowpack reflected this above normal rainfall on the San Joaquin Valley floor.

A major winter storm system produced significant rainfall and blizzard like conditions across Southern California.Rainfall totals up to two inches were measured along the coast while mountain areas below 6000 feet receivedup to 5 inches of rain. Mudslides closed Interstate 14 near Santa Clarita. At elevations above 6000 feet, strongwinds 40 to 50 MPH combined with snow to produce blizzard-like conditions. Snow accumulations up to six inches were reported at higher elevations.
The San Bernardino County Sheriff reported roof damage and downed road signs due to strong winds.

High winds downed numerous trees and power lines, especially from Garberville north to Orick.  A falling tree damaged a car in Blue Lake.  Some peak speeds:  45 mph 6E Garberville, 84 mph Cooskie Mtn, 74 mph Maple Creek, 51 mph 1.5N Arcata, 54 mph 1W Orick.

Mudslides closed Interstate 14 near Santa Clarita. At elevations above 6000 feet, strongwinds 40 to 50 MPH combined with snow to produce blizzard-like conditions. Snow accumulations up to six inches were reported at higher elevations.

Snow accumulations up to six inches were reported at higher elevations.

Heavy thunderstorm rains produced flood waters which caused damage to parts of US Highway 95 between Needles, CA and Vidal Junction, CA.  Eight vehicles were stranded by flooded washes running across the highway and one car was caught in the flood waters.  No injuries occurred, but the 43 mile stretch of road was closed for a week to repair the damage.

Widespread flooding of creeks and streams in Del Norte and Humboldt Counties.  More than $1.3M in damage in Ferndale when a muddy torrent flowed through downtown at 2pm on the 8th.  Many road closures around Crescent City, Eureka and Arcata.  All routes into NW California were cut at one time.  Rainfall at the NWS in Eureka - 24 hour total 5.04 inches - new all time record.  Hiouchi (7NW Crescent City) received 9.44 inches on the 8th.
72 mph wind at Cooskie Mountain.  Branches were broken off near Carlotta.
Strong winds once again caused numerous tree and power line downings.  Tree limbs 1.5 inches in diameter were snapping near Honeydew.  Some peak speeds:  87 mph Cooskie Mountain, 75 mph 9S Honeydew
High winds once again fell numerous trees and power lines.  Some peak gusts:  60 mph 8N Laytonville, 55 mph Maple Creek.
A weather spotter in Truckee measured 8 inches of snow in about a 12 hour period.

A weather spotter in the town of Mammoth Lakes reported 10 inches of new snow since the previous day at about 8,000 feet.  The 24 hour snowfall ending in the early morning at the Mammoth Ski Resort was about 2 feet.
The Mammoth Ski Resort received 12-18 inches of snow in about a 10 hour period.

A cold front sweeping through the San Bernardino and Riverside Mountains generated strong winds and heavy, wet snow in elevations above 6000 feet.  Snow began falling during the early morning hours of the 22nd, and increased in intensity, along with the winds, until blizzard conditions were reported shortly after 7:00 AM. In the Big Bear Valley, 40 to 50 mph winds combined with heavy snow to snap trees and power lines, and cause severe visibility restrictions for several hours.  By the early afternoon, the storm had diminished, leaving up to one half foot of snow in some areas.
Swift Creek near Carville flooded.

Rain slickened roads led to a vehicle sliding into the deepened Merced River near El Portal.  One fatality resulted from drowning as the river was unusually high following a "wet" month in the county.

A powerful winter storm ravaged the Greater Lake Tahoe area over a two and a half day period.  The main portion of the storm dumped heavy snow over the region from late Friday night (20th) into early Sunday morning (22nd).  The cold moist flow continued into early Monday morning, but snowfall amounts were not as significant as on Saturday into early Sunday.  Some noteworthy snowfall amounts over the 12-24 hour period from late Friday night into Saturday night were as follows:  30 inches of snow at Tahoe City, Donner Summit (7200 feet I-80), and Incline Village, 40-50 inches of snow at Kirkwood Ski Resort, 60 inches at Echo Summit, and 48-60 inches around the Truckee area.  Storm totals ending early Monday morning (23rd) were phenomenal.  Much of the region below 7000 feet received between 4 and 6 feet of snow.  Some ski resorts reported record or near record snow amounts for the storm.  Alpine Meadows reported a total of 100 inches at 9800 feet.  In addition, the 81 inches of snow that had fallen at 7000 feet at Alpine Meadows broke 12, 24, 48, and 72 hour records.  Finally, about 8 feet of snow fell at the Kirkwood Ski Resort in a 36 hour period, 6 inches shy of the 1982 record.The powerful winter storm immobilized holiday travelers.  Many roads were closed, including I-80 and U.S. Highway 50.  There were numerous traffic accidents as well as stranded motorists.  Power was out to as much as 63,000 customers.  A roof of a portable classroom collasped under the weight of the heavy snow on Saturday, causing damage to school items.  Finally, there were some deaths attributed to the storm.  A cross country skier and a snowboarder were found dead over the weekend.  They were trapped and disoriented by the heavy snowfall.

Mudslides closed Interstate 14 near Santa Clarita. At elevations above 6000 feet, strongwinds 40 to 50 MPH combined with snow to produce blizzard-like conditions. Snow accumulations up to six inches were reported at higher elevations.
A moist Pacific Storm dumped heavy snow overnight in the Sierra Nevada Mountains above the Owens Valley.  Aspendell received at least 12 inches of new snow.

This winter storm produced accumulations of 18 inches and more in the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada and about 5 inches in the Lake Tahoe basin.  In the Shasta-Cascades, snow fell at the 1500 foot level, causing the closure of Interstate 5 during the evening of the 4th. In Lake county, 166 customers lost power as trees laden with snow fell on power lines the night of the 4th.

Rains of 2 to 4.5 inches across El Dorado county during the early morning brought many small streams to above flood stage.  On Deer Creek near the city of Cameron Park, 10 homes were evacuated.  Further downstream, in eastern Sacramento county, the creek inundated the local area, damaging a restaurant in an historic building in the town of Sloughhouse.  The city of El Dorado's post office ended up with 4 feet of water inside the building from Slate Creek.

Following frontal passage through Central California, an unstable airmass brought convective activity to the area this data.  This small tornado, the first of four this day, was reported late to the office but eyewitness accounts and damage lead to the assessment of its being an F1 on the damage intensity scale.  Its path was northwest to southeast with a length of 1/4 mile through pasture and farm land except for an encounter with a farm house's garage.  The garage roof was completely removed.

Significant rainfall in the Southern Sierra Nevada brought high stream flows resulting in temporary flooding in the Yosemite National Park area.  Highways 120/140/41 had traffic limited to ascertain the effect of high water in the area.  No permanent damage was reported to infrastructure as several streams and the Merced River spilled water onto mountain area roadways.

A Tulare County thunderstorm produced a lightning strike on a barn that stampeded cattle.  Fleeing from the thunder the cattle crossed into a roadway and subsequently were struck by a truck tractor with 17 of the cattle killed.
This event occurred simultaneously with the tornado in Merced County well to the north.  Due to the time of year no crop damage occurred and no other damage reports from the public were noted.

Strong Santa Ana winds toppled trees and electric poles, smashed windows, blew out signs, and knocked out power to tens of thousands across scattered areas of Southern California.  In Orange County, gusts at Fremont Canyon were measured as high as 111 mph.  Two deaths occured in San Bernardino County, both in Fontana.  A 28 year old man died when a live powerline was blown on him and another 82 year old man died when a pine tree branch fell onto his van, breaking his neck.  Two minor injuries occurred in Huntington Central Park in Orange County, when a 60 foot tree blew down.  Also in San Diego County, one minor injury resulted when a tree crashed into a car traveling on Highway 78, just east of Ramona.  Just below Crestline, in the San Bernardino Mountains, a radio tower was blown down and the roof blown off the transmitter building.  Mira Loma was particularly hard hit with many downed trees and 50 power poles.  Two tractor-trailer rigs were flipped on the I-15 near Devore, closing this major north-south highway for 15 hours, while a pickup with camper was blown over on Highway 70 near Santa Isabel.  Several cars were smashed under falling trees, up to 9 feet in diameter, and downed power lines sparked fires that were fanned by the gusty winds.  In Rialto where winds were clocked at 92 mph, sparks from downed power lines started a blaze that gutted a tile production plant.  In San Diego County, the winds knocked  over one and one half million pounds of avocadoes from the trees, a large percentage of which could not be marketed.
A weather spotter in the town of Mammoth Lakes reported 12 inches of snow in about a 12 hour period.  15 inches of snow was reported at the Mammoth Ski Resort.
A wind sensor in Truckee measured sustained winds of 52 kts.
A weather spotter in the town of Doyle measured sustained winds of 35 kts.

Surface high pressure across the Great Basin produced strong SantaAna winds across the mountains and valleys of Southern California. Sustained wind speeds of 35 to 50 mph with gusts up to 70 mph were common.

Strong Santa Ana winds once again ravaged Southern California. Sustained northeast winds 40 to 50 MPH withgusts up to 70 MPH were reported. Thousands were left without power as the winds snapped power lines. Inaddition, blowing debris and downed trees caused widespread damage.
Deans Creek near Rio Dell, Janes Creek in Arcata, many creeks near Carlotta and other small streams were flooding.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY COASTS INCLUDING DOWNTOWN LOS ANGELES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm system produced significant rainfall and blizzard like conditions across Southern California.Rainfall totals up to two inches were measured along the coast while mountain areas below 6000 feet receivedup to 5 inches of rain. Mudslides closed Interstate 14 near Santa Clarita. At elevations above 6000 feet, strongwinds 40 to 50 MPH combined with snow to produce blizzard-like conditions. Snow accumulations up to six inches were reported at higher elevations.

The heaviest snow in many years caused a number of problems in the Shasta-Cascades region and in the Sierra Nevada range.  8-foot snow accumulations at the higher elevations in a 36-hour timeframe came within half a foot of the record at Kirkwood Ski Resort (Amador/El Dorado counties).  A storm total of 8 feet fell in the Lake Tahoe area.  4-foot accumulations in 30 hours occurred at the 3000 to 4000-foot elevations.  Heavy snow fell as low as 1500 feet, and lighter accumulations fell as low as 500 feet MSL.  The heavy snow pulled down numerous trees, taking power lines with them.  About 120,000 people in the Sierra Nevada and its foothills lost power for several days, with some getting back power late Christmas Eve, and most regaining power by the day after Christmas.  The snow closed most roads and several major highways in the region, including state route 70, Interstate 80 (closed nearly two days), and US Route 50 (closed nearly 3 days).  The passenger train "California Zephyr" (Amtrak's Oakland to Chicago route) derailed in the Sierra Nevada near the town of Blue Canyon.  Falling trees brought down a water flume in Tuolumne county and stopped power to a water pump that serviced much of Amador county; so county residents also went without running water for several days.  Small avalanches at a ski resort injured two people. The storm also caused two fatalities.  One man died from hypothermia when he got lost cross-country skiing in the blizzard condtions.  A woman died near Lassen Lodge when her car plummeted off a 150-foot cliff from state highway 36.  These heavy snows would not melt until the end of the year, when they contributed to some of the worst flooding in state history.
An avalanche Saturday morning on the Face Run at Heavenly Ski Resort sent at least two people to the hospital.  One victim suffered from a broken knee.  The other victim suffered from a back injury.

Weather spotter and law enforcement officials reported the following heavy snow amounts in the Lassen/Eastern Plumas area in the last 24 hours:  8-12 inches in the Susanville area and 14 inches at Portola.

Blizzard conditions developed across parts of Mono County from 3 PM to 9 PM.  Wind gusts of 61 to as high as 87 kts were reported in the Mammoth Lakes area.  A tree fell on a house in Mammoth Lakes.  Another tree fell on a power line, knocking power out for over half the town.  Fifty cars were reported stranded in the blizzard Sunday evening on Highway 203 between U.S. 395 and Mammoth Lakes.
Heavy seas capsized a fishing vessel off of Fern Canyon when it was caught abeam by a large wave.  Two crew members were rescued but one drown.

A broad trof immediately along the California Coast supported significant rain event for the San Joaquin Valley and adjacent Southern Sierra Nevada.  Small streams ran high and while not quite meeting flash flooding criteria posed a serious risk to Mariposa County cleanup crews working in and around Burns Creek.  Storm total amounts from late on the 8th through early on 11th ... rain near 7 inches in the 2-3K foot elevation of adjacent Madera County.  Central San Joaquin Valley storm total rain amounts were over 2 inches along the east side ... near 1 inch on the west side.  In the Lake Isabella area of Kern County almost 3 inches of rain occurred from the storm.

CZ_NAME: SALINAS VALLEY/INTERIOR MONTEREY COUNTY/SAN BENITO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along with heavy rain on the San Joaquin Valley floor substantial snow accumulations were recorded in the adjacent Southern Sierra Nevada Mountains.  These amounts were particularly large considering the time of year.  In Fresno County 27 Inches of snow was noted at Edison Lake from the event...15 inches at Shaver Lake.  Similar amounts at Wishon Dam...Kaiser Pass...and Huntington Lake.
Following a significant rain over Central California the post-frontal airmass became unstable.  Convective activity resulted in funnel clouds and at least one small tornado.
Thunderstorm downburst wind resulted in small structure roof damage in west Fresno County.  Some confusion whether it was a small tornado but interviews lack any indication of observed rotation.
Thunderstorm with 1/2 to 3/4 inch hail in east Visalia.  Rained 0.92 inches within an hour.  Street flooding.
Urban flooding as the result of rain, associated with thunderstorms and locally heavy, throughout the afternoon hours.  Rained as much as 1.54 inches in 2 hours between 1350-1550 PST at one location.

Runoff from the previous days heavy rains and severe weather progressed toward the northwest Fresno County town of Mendota during the night.  Over the saturated farm ground, water reached Mendota early in the morning of the 13th causing local flooding and silting of city streets.  Rain amounts reported included 1 inch in an hour ending at 1730 PST on the west side the previous afternoon.  Damage to agriculture was to irrigation and water conveyance systems upstream from Mendota.  Minor damage to houses along southside of Mendota from high water.
Hail bouncing and covering streets of downtown Visalia.  Heavy rain brought water over the curb tops along with the hail.
Hail reported by California Highway Patrol but size was unavailable.  No damage was reported.
Spotter report of 1/2 inch hail over a 1/2 mile swath in the vicnity of Clovis and Manning Avenues southeast of Fresno.
Funnel observed in rural area. Formed and dissipated in 5 minutes.

Surface high pressure, building into the Great Basin, produced strong Santa Ana winds across the mountains and valleys of SouthernCalifornia. Rialto airport reported northeast wind gusts of 50 mph.In Ontario, several power lines were knocked down. In Corona andHemet, numerous branches and small trees were blown down.
Weather observer at Los Angeles International Airport reported a waterspout over the Santa Monica Bay.
Heavy rainfall in the vicinity of Hanford brought 0.34 Inches in only a 20' period.  With flat ground poor drainage posed problems for urban areas in the Central San Joaquin Valley.
Wind gusts to 51 mph at Crescent City airport at 11 am PDT.
Wind gust to 46 mph at Crescent City airport at 6 am PDT.
Wind gusts with a strong Pacific cold front reached 50 mph at Bunker Hill (9am PDT), 45 mph at Trinidad Head (9am), 56 mph at Cooskie Mountain (3pm) and 61 mph at Maple Creek at 3pm.
Funnel was observed over the city of Visalia down to about 200 feet above ground level.  Associated with thunderstorm producing heavy rain and hail.
A local television station's news camera crew observed three funnel clouds along State Highway 99.
A large accumulation of hail caused a number of automobile accidents including 7 minor injuries in the city of Redding.

A strong cold front moved through Northern California, producing scattered power outages and minor flooding in the Sacramento Valley, and snowfall and drifting snow in the Sierra Nevada that caused closure of Interstate 80. Gusts to 80 mph were observed along I-80 at the 6000 foot elevation.
Weather spotter observed several cold-air funnel clouds.

Relatively high gradient wind for the middle of summer brought wind of 28 to 40 MPH to the west side of the San Joaquin Valley on this day.  While not meeting high wind criteria, gusty wind did break a limb from an old oak tree in southeast Bakersfield, Kern County.  The limb fell and killed a little girl as she stood beneath the tree.

A strong cold front moved through Northern California, producing scattered power outages and minor flooding in the Sacramento Valley, and snowfall and drifting snow in the Sierra Nevada that caused closure of Interstate 80. Gusts to 80 mph were observed along I-80 at the 6000 foot elevation.
A thunderstorm gust front produced wind speeds up to 65 mph in the Coachella Valley. The winds knocked out power to over 4800residents and blew down numerous small trees.
Two people were slightly injured when lightning struck their umbrella.
Funnel cloud was reported northwest of Hemet.
Weather observer at March Air Force Base reported 2 funnel cloudssouthwest of the base.
Funnel cloud was reported just west of Century City.
Lightning struck a man in Calabasas when he was leaving work forthe afternoon.
Heavy rains of 4 to 6 inches fell over the higher elevations of the Coastal range
Roof Torn off a house and numerous trees blown down
2-4 inches of rain fell in coastal mountains, some urban and small stream flooding
4 to 6 inches of rain in the San Luis Obispo Co. Mountains caused Urban and small stream flooding and associated mudslides in the steep terrain and along Hwy 1 and 101.

Widespread Flash Flooding on Small Rivers and Creeks such as Manzantas Creek in Santa Rosa. The Russian River also rose near but did not exceed Flood stage. 24 hour rainfall amounts were in the 4 to 6 inch range. over Sonoma County.
Dense coastal fog spread over Northwest California from Pt. Arena northward to the Oregon border.
Waterspout was reported by the Coast Guard off of coast from Arcata Airport.
One half inch of sleet or soft hail covered the ground down to sea level south of Eureka at Humboldt Hill.  Caused several vehicle accidents on US 101.  Upto 5 inches of snow fell on coastal hills.
Up to 4 inches of snow fell on the coastal hills with 2 inches down as low as 1200 feet.
This small F0 tornado demolished a small farm structure and shredded bushes along its path.
This small F0 tornado ran S-to-N through a residential area of Hanford.  Small trees were toppled, lawn furniture lifted and deposited into a pool, fences knocked down, and trash cans displaced.
This funnel was observed by trained spotters to have reached within 200 feet above ground level with a well defined wall cloud.  No damage reported.
Observers noted this funnel cloud reaching within 200 feet above ground.  No damage reported.

Over the four-day period, a series of storms dumped about 5 feet of snow at the 7000 foot level.  The major highways, US route 50, Interstate 80, and State Route 88 were closed for a few hours on the 21st, the heaviest precipitation day.
Strong northwest winds, following the passage of a cold front, werereported in the mountains of Southern California. Sustained winds40 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph were measured.
Surface high pressure over the Great Basin produced Santa Ana winds across the mountains and valleys of Southern Cailfornia. Sustained wind speeds 35 to 45 mph with gusts up to 70 mph were reported.
Surface high pressure over the Great Basin produced Santa Ana winds across the mountains and valleys of Southern Cailfornia. Sustained wind speeds 35 to 45 mph with gusts up to 70 mph were reported.

Dry, gusty Santa Ana winds helped fuel a fire in the Los Padres National Forest. A low voltage electrical line failed, igniting the blaze. Over 10,000 acres were burned. Two firefighters were injured during the fire. By the end of the month, the fire was about 40 percent contained.
Santa Ana winds helped spread a fire in Riverside county. Over 300acres of land was burned with one home destroyed.

Strong gradient winds, with widespread gusts over 60 mph, blew through the Antelope Valley.  More than 60 power poles were toppled at Rosamond and a car was severely damaged by a downed tree.  Also, a few roofs were damaged and several windows were blown out.

A closed, cold-core low passed over the Central San Joaquin Valley at the time of maximum heating on Tuesday, March 12.  Numerous thunderstorms developed with some reaching severe limits. Some cells spawned tornado activity.  Agricultural losses are difficult to assess due to the early stages of crop development, including the blooming/leafing almond crop.  This particular event was the intial one as reported by a Riverdale City Fireman.

This storm continued for three days on the 25,26,27. winds in the 40 kt range were widespread over the San Francisco Bay Area. There were some gusts in the 50 kt range over higher terrain. The major result was 42,000 power outages spred over the period. Most of this was caused by trees or limbs falling across power lines. In many instances the lines simply were blown to close to each and they arced. A couple of small fires resulted from down power lines.

The fire known as Wild Cattle Canyon Fire burned over 20,000 acres mostly in the Las Padres National Forest and Big Sur area. This fire lasted 7 days and was supported by an IMET from San Juaoquin Valley NWFO. 15 Federal, State, and local agencies provided as many as 2200 firefighters were on the fire.Three Firefighter were injured from falling in the steep terrain. The cost in the firefight alone was $4.5 Million. 5 homes were distroyed. two vehicles,two travel trailers and nine outbuildings were distroyed. High temperatures proceeded the fires with a high of 102 in Monterey. Heavy fog caused problems because aircraft and helicopters could not operate over the very rugged terrain. A light drizzle finally came and aided the demise of the fire but also made the terrain very slippery and dangerous for firefighters.
1.48 inches of rain fell in Redwood city. The rain accumulated on the roof of the Office Depot causing it to collapse.
One large 1 " hail and a number of smaller hail reports were made as storm crossed S. Marin county headed toward the San Francisco Bay.  Reported broken winshields could not be proven.

This fire known as the Big Sur fire began Oct 18 near Ventana Camp Park. Much of the fire occurred in the Ventana wilderness area. 2500 Fire fighter participated. 4400 acres were burned. light rain helped control fire. The cost of fighting the fire is estimated 12.3 million. 180 fire engines were used. 13 helicopters and 3 air tankers were used.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Ana winds averaging 30 mph, with gusts to 45, fanned the flames of three wildfires across San Diego County.  The first fire started in Harmony Grove, and quickly spread westward to eastern sections of Carlsbad and Encinitas, burning some 8,600 acres, destroying 125 homes, damaging 122 others, and seriously burning one man. Another fire broke out at 6:55 pm on the Rincon Indian Reservation, and spread to Valley Center, consuming 1,800 acres, and destroying six structures.  The largest fire began around 2:00 am on the 22nd, on desolate Otay Mountain, and spread to the Tijuana River in Mexico, burning over 14,000 acres, three radio towers, and six cows, before it was under control on the 25th.  In all, six people were injured, one critically by fire, 24,435 acres burned, and preliminary damages totaled 37.4 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Merchandise and pieces of tarpaulin were sent flying through the air, as a dust devil developed at a swap meet in the city of San Diego.  One woman was taken to the hospital with a leg injury, and several booths were knocked down.

Thunderstorms provided for gusty wind and small hail in Central California including this event through northern Kern County where hail was reported at 1/2 inch in diameter and covering a major roadway at a point between Bakersfield and Porterville.
Hail not quite reaching severe limits occurred most likely with the tornadic thunderstorm that occurred in near southwest Fresno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast wind known as Mono Winds ripped through the middle Southern Sierra Nevada Mountains late Saturday October 26 and early Sunday October 27.  Known damage limited to large trees down within Yosemite National Park.  Observed winds from USFS RAWS stations ... Oak Opening 020 degs at 57Kts Sunday Oct 27 at 0600 PST; peak wind of 85Kts overnight at Oak Opening at 3200 feet in Tulare County.

The first storm of the 1996-97 water year brought record amounts of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and adjacent Southern Sierra Nevada Mountains.  Rain on the 29th in Fresno was 1.5 inches topping the old mark of 0.87 inches for the date.  Over the 29th and 30th almost 2 inches of rain fell.  27 inches of snow fell at Edison Lake with 15 inches at Shaver Lake, both in the Fresno County Southern Sierra Nevada.

In the unstable air behind a cold front, a spotter witnessed a funnel cloud over the town of Woodland.
A winter storm produced heavy snow across the San Bernardino Mountains. Angelus Oaks reported 12 inches while Forest Falls and Green Valley Lake reported 8 inches.
A week-long stretch of temperatures over 100 degrees, topping at 110 degrees for two days, combined with higher-than-normal relative humidities led to 4 heat-related deaths in Kern County.

Strong winds from a Pacific storm system caused numerous trees and power lines to go down.  A carport was destroyed by the wind and several buildings were damaged by falling trees.  Peak gust of 77 mph at Trinidad Head and 58 mph in Arcata.
Widespread convection producing hail, mostly pea-sized.

Crab boat Ocean Gypsy was found by US Coast Guard helicopter in the surf of Mad River Beach.  The two fishermen on board were missing and presumed lost (one body was eventually found).  The exact time of the incident cannot be determined but occured between 0700 PST and 1430 PST.  Incident probably weather related due to strong winds and rough surf.
